components.label: Web/Desktop/Mobile Client
components.description: Maintains a persistent WebSocket connection to the gateway node; handles reconnection with exponential backoff

components.label: Sticky Load Balancer
components.description: Routes WebSocket connections using consistent hashing on user_id — ensures reconnects land on the same gateway node without a cache lookup

components.label: WebSocket Gateway / Chat Server
components.description: Stateful server holding active WebSocket connections; subscribes to pub/sub channels for each workspace the user belongs to

components.label: Pub/Sub Broker (Kafka / Redis Pub/Sub)
components.description: Fan-out backbone — message written once, broadcast to all gateway nodes subscribed to that channel; decouples sender from recipient delivery

components.label: Message Store (Cassandra, sharded by workspace_id)
components.description: Append-only wide-column store; partition key is (workspace_id, channel_id), cluster key is message_id DESC for efficient range reads

components.label: Hot Cache (Redis)
components.description: Stores last 100 messages per channel, channel membership lists, unread counts, and presence TTL keys — eliminates DB reads on the hot path

connections.label: Publish message event to channel topic
Fan-out to all gateway nodes subscribed to channel
Update hot message cache + presence TTL
Read last 100 messages on channel open

keyInsights: Sticky load balancing by user_id means a reconnecting client always lands on the same gateway node — no round-trip to presence cache to find which server holds the connection
The pub/sub broker is the fan-out engine: a 10K-member channel requires the message to be delivered to potentially thousands of gateway nodes, each of which pushes to their connected clients
Cassandra sharded by workspace_id gives strong tenant isolation — all messages for a workspace live on the same shard set, making cross-tenant queries structurally impossible
Cursor-based pagination uses (channel_id, message_id DESC) composite index — the client passes the oldest message_id it has loaded, the server returns the next page; no OFFSET, O(log n) at any depth
Presence TTL: client heartbeats every 30s call EXPIRE(user:presence:{id}, 35s) — if the client disconnects, the key expires naturally within 35s with no explicit DELETE or socket-close handler

tradeoffs.decision: Cassandra over PostgreSQL for message storage
tradeoffs.pro: Linear write scalability, time-series optimised, partition-per-workspace gives natural isolation
tradeoffs.con: No joins or transactions; eventual consistency requires idempotent message delivery; complex operational model

tradeoffs.decision: Redis Pub/Sub over Kafka for fan-out
tradeoffs.pro: Sub-millisecond latency, zero operational overhead for small deployments
tradeoffs.con: No message persistence or replay — if a gateway node is down during delivery, the message is lost from pub/sub; Cassandra is the source of truth for recovery

tradeoffs.decision: Sticky load balancing over stateless routing
tradeoffs.pro: Eliminates per-message presence cache lookup; simpler gateway code
tradeoffs.con: Uneven load distribution if some users send far more messages; requires consistent hashing to rebalance gracefully when nodes are added or removed

antiPatterns: Polling the database for new messages — at 5B messages/day this would generate 300K DB queries/sec just to check for updates
Storing presence state in the database — presence changes thousands of times per second; a relational DB cannot absorb this write rate
Using OFFSET for channel history pagination — OFFSET forces a full sequential scan of all prior rows; at channel depth of 1M messages this is catastrophically slow
Fan-out by querying channel membership on every message send — membership lists must live in cache, not the DB, to avoid a DB round-trip for every message
